summary: Gruber reflects on seeing Steve Jobs up close after the WWDC 2011 keynote, just months before Jobs's death. He describes Jobs as looking impossibly ancient yet with eyes still blazing with intensity. The central image is the grass stains on Jobs's well-worn New Balance sneakers — fresh, bright green stains that suggested Jobs had recently taken a walk somewhere beautiful despite the intensity of keynote preparation. Gruber interprets the grass stains as evidence of Jobs's legendary ability to focus on what matters and ignore what doesn't. After Jobs's death, Gruber returns to this image and constructs a hopeful story: that Jobs made time for a peaceful walk with his family in his final months.
key_points: Jobs looked impossibly old and ancient at WWDC 2011, but his eyes retained their famous intensity
The grass stains on Jobs's sneakers revealed his ability to prioritize what mattered — keynote preparation didn't consume his entire life
Jobs's gift was knowing what to give a shit about, and grass stains on his shoes didn't make the cut
The grass stains become a metaphor for limited time, well spent — choosing life over work even while preparing his final keynote
money_quotes: He looked old. Not old in a way that could be measured in years or even decades, but impossibly old.
Not tired, but weary; not ill or unwell, but rather, somehow, ancient. But not his eyes. His eyes were young and bright, their weapons-grade intensity intact.
One of Jobs's many gifts was that he knew what to give a shit about.
He knew how to focus and prioritize his time and attention. Grass stains on his sneakers didn't make the cut.
Those grass stained sneakers were the product of limited time, well spent.
I like to think that in the run-up to his final keynote, Steve made time for a long, peaceful walk. Somewhere beautiful, where there are no footpaths and the grass grows thick.
key_insight: A single observed detail — grass stains on Steve Jobs's sneakers at his final keynote — becomes a profound meditation on mortality, priorities, and the wisdom of knowing what deserves your attention.
